skill.name: zlibrary-to-notebooklm
skill.description: Automates downloading accessible Z-Library book pages and uploading PDF or converted EPUB files into Google NotebookLM.
skill.summary: Download a Z-Library book file, convert EPUB when needed, and create a NotebookLM source.
skill.author: zstmfhy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-counted many Markdown command examples and non-cryptographic metadata as executable or cryptographic issues. However, the Python upload workflow confirms unsafe shell=True command construction with file paths and titles, persistent browser session storage, external network automation, and filesystem writes, so the skill is high risk and should not be published without remediation.

high_findings.title: Unsafe shell command construction from file names and titles
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: scripts/upload.py builds shell strings for Python and NotebookLM CLI commands and executes them with shell=True. The file path and notebook title can be influenced by downloaded file names or derived metadata, so a crafted quote or shell metacharacter could cause command injection.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly interpolates Path and title values into shell command strings and passes shell=True. The values originate from downloaded file names and derived titles, which are not safely escaped for shell execution.

high_findings.title: Persistent browser session data and optional credential file
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The workflow creates ~/.zlibrary, saves Playwright browser storage state, reuses a persistent browser profile, and can read a config.json containing email and password. Permissions are restricted for the main storage file, but the skill still handles sensitive session material.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files explicitly describe and implement storage of cookies, browser profile data, and optional credentials. This is expected for the workflow but is a real sensitive-data handling risk.

medium_findings.title: External browser automation downloads and uploads user content
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally navigates to Z-Library URLs, downloads content, then uploads files to NotebookLM through a CLI. This matches the advertised purpose, but users must understand that book content and session state are processed through external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and skill instructions clearly implement the network workflow. No hidden exfiltration was found, but external transfer is central to the skill.

medium_findings.title: Local filesystem writes to Downloads and temporary paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Downloaded files are saved to the user Downloads directory, converted Markdown is written under /tmp, and large sources are split into sibling Markdown files. This is functional behavior, but it can leave sensitive book content on disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and necessary for conversion and upload. The risk is data residue rather than malicious file access.

low_findings.title: Markdown command examples misclassified as Ruby backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many external_commands hits are Markdown inline code or fenced shell examples in documentation and SKILL.md, not executable code paths. They can guide users to run commands, but they are not Ruby backtick execution by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation, and the detected backticks are formatting delimiters or command examples. The actual executable shell risk is separately confirmed in scripts/upload.py.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detections are metadata false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts point to package and skill metadata lines such as names, descriptions, versions, or command labels. No cryptographic operation or hash algorithm use was found at those cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive metadata, not crypto API usage. This strongly indicates a pattern-match false positive.

low_findings.title: High entropy detections appear caused by multilingual prose
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-entropy heuristic flagged Markdown files containing Chinese prose and mixed punctuation. No encoded payload, encrypted blob, or prompt injection attempt was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are human-readable documentation with substantial non-ASCII text. Targeted searches found no instruction override language or hidden payload evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with shell=True and interpolated values
dangerous_patterns.description: Dangerous pattern confirmed in the upload workflow. Replace shell strings with argument lists and validate titles and paths before invoking NotebookLM or conversion commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The repeated pattern uses command strings and shell=True with values derived at runtime. This is a well-known command injection class.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent browser profile containing session material
dangerous_patterns.description: Dangerous pattern confirmed for shared or unmanaged environments. The workflow stores browser cookies, local storage, session storage, and profile data under ~/.zlibrary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code and workflow documentation explicitly create and reuse session-bearing files. Permissions reduce exposure but do not remove the sensitive-data handling concern.

content.value_statement: Researchers often need a book source inside NotebookLM before they can ask reliable questions. This skill automates login reuse, file download, EPUB conversion, NotebookLM creation, and source upload.
actual_capabilities: Uses Playwright to open a Z-Library book URL and trigger a PDF or EPUB download.
Stores and reuses a local Z-Library browser session under the user home directory.
Converts EPUB files to Markdown with ebooklib and BeautifulSoup.
Splits very large Markdown files before uploading them to NotebookLM.
Calls the NotebookLM CLI to create a notebook and add downloaded sources.
Returns notebook and source identifiers after a successful upload.
limitations: Requires existing legal access to the source material and an authenticated Z-Library session.
Requires Playwright, Chromium, ebooklib, BeautifulSoup, and the NotebookLM CLI to be installed.
May fail if Z-Library or NotebookLM changes page structure or command output.
Uses browser automation and shell commands, so it needs careful review before shared environments.

use_cases.title: Build a Study Notebook
use_cases.description: Upload a legally accessible book into NotebookLM for chapter summaries and study questions.

use_cases.title: Prepare Literature Notes
use_cases.description: Convert a research book or long EPUB into a NotebookLM source for structured reading notes.

use_cases.title: Create Team Reading Sources
use_cases.description: Create NotebookLM notebooks from approved internal reading material for later discussion.

prompt_templates.title: Upload One Book
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup with a known book URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload this legally accessible Z-Library book to NotebookLM and tell me the notebook ID: [book URL]

prompt_templates.title: Check Setup First
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this workspace has the tools needed for the Z-Library to NotebookLM workflow, then list any missing setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Convert and Split EPUB
prompt_templates.scenario: Handle a long EPUB that may exceed upload limits
prompt_templates.prompt: Download this authorized EPUB source, convert it for NotebookLM, split it if needed, and upload each part: [book URL]

prompt_templates.title: Run With Security Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced use in a sensitive environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the planned commands, confirm which files and accounts will be used, then run the upload only after showing me the risks.

output_examples.input: Upload a legally accessible Z-Library book URL to NotebookLM.
output_examples.output: The skill reports that the download completed, a NotebookLM notebook was created, and the source was uploaded with returned identifiers.

output_examples.input: Upload a very large EPUB source.
output_examples.output: The skill converts the EPUB to Markdown, splits the content into parts, uploads each part, and reports how many parts succeeded.

output_examples.input: Run without a saved Z-Library session.
output_examples.output: The skill stops and asks the user to complete the one-time browser login before retrying the upload workflow.

best_practices: Use only public domain, licensed, personally owned, or otherwise authorized materials.
Run the workflow in a local user account, not on shared production machines.
Review generated file names and NotebookLM titles before executing upload commands.
anti_patterns: Do not use the workflow to bypass copyright or access restrictions.
Do not run it with untrusted book URLs or file names in a privileged shell.
Do not share the saved browser profile or storage_state file with other users.

faq.question: Does this skill download books automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses browser automation to download a PDF or EPUB from a provided Z-Library book page.

faq.question: Does it require a Z-Library account?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow expects a saved local browser session before automatic download can run.

faq.question: Can it upload EPUB files directly?
faq.answer: It converts EPUB files to Markdown before upload because NotebookLM support and size limits may vary.

faq.question: Does it store credentials?
faq.answer: It stores browser session data under the user home directory and may read a local config file if present.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared servers?
faq.answer: No. It launches a browser, stores session files, writes downloads, and runs shell commands.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but local dependencies must still be installed.
